output.summary_final: {"title": "Building Trails That Last", "bullets": ["A good maintenance routine is short and boring: check bolts, measure chain wear, clean stanchions, and inspect pads.", "For climbs, a consistent cadence reduces drivetrain load and makes small traction changes easier to manage.", "When conditions are wet, the best skill is decision-making: knowing when to back off to protect traction and trails.", "Tire pressure and casing choice often matter more than a minor suspension tweak.", "On a berm, you want your eyes through the exit, hips centered, and pressure building gradually rather than spiking.", "Most mystery creaks are solved by cleaning contact surfaces, re-greasing threads, and re-torquing to spec.", "The bike should feel quiet and predictable so your attention stays on the trail, not on noises or surprises."]

{"title": "Designing Healthy Systems", "bullets": ["A good on-call rotation should wake up rarely and respond obviously.", "Secrets handling improves with short-lived, scoped tokens and automation.", "Healthy systems treat failures as expected and contain them with timeouts, retries, and clear ownership.", "Writing things down turns disagreement into collaboration by pointing at assumptions instead of people.", "Alerting should be action-oriented if no one can take an immediate action.", "Security is strongest when the secure path is the easiest path, not enforced by shame or heroics.", "Error budgets work when they change behavior, not charts that are ignored during planning."]

{"title": "Wreck Diving Fundamentals", "bullets": ["The calmest diver is often the safest diver because calm keeps breathing slow and decisions deliberate.", "For navigation, pick one primary reference and one backup: heading plus depth or reef contour plus current direction.", "Good buoyancy control protects the environment and also reduces task load for navigation and photography.", "Neutral buoyancy drills—hovering and slow fin pivots—make everything else easier when conditions get choppy.", "A wreck can feel like a hallway, but silt can turn it into a blank room in seconds if you fin hard.", "The most valuable skill is the ability to call a dive early without ego.", "Dive plans work best when they are simple, shared, and include clear turn pressures and exit routes.", "Start simple and repeat it: one habit listeners can try this week to improve at wreck diving"]}

{"title": "Mastering Underwater Photography", "bullets": ["Labeling files and writing captions the same day prevents memory gaps that ruin long-term projects.", "A small strobe angle change can reduce backscatter dramatically by moving the beam away from the lens axis.", "In mixed lighting, decide the dominant color temperature and then correct everything else relative to that decision.", "One-light practice teaches you more than a complicated kit because it forces you to learn placement.", "Documentary work is built on routines: backups, notes, and small habits that prevent catastrophic loss.", "A grid is less about softness and more about spill; it keeps light off areas that should stay dark."]}

{"title": "Index Investing Without Myths", "bullets": ["Risk is not the same as volatility; risk is the chance of not meeting goals or suffering permanent loss.", "Fees compound quietly, lowering costs is like increasing returns without taking additional risk.", "Position sizing rules prevent one great idea from becoming a portfolio-ending mistake.", "Drawdowns are math and emotion: a 50% loss needs a 100% gain to recover, which changes timelines.", "Diversification that actually diversifies means different drivers, not just different tickers that move together.", "A simple stress test asks: can you afford higher rates, longer vacancies, and unexpected repairs without panic selling?"]}
